responseSet.title: Mind, Brain, Body: Toward a Convergence of Psychoanalysis and Neurobiology: By Morton F. Reiser.  New York:  Basic Books.  1984.  Pp. 228.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mind, Brain, Body: Toward a Convergence of Psychoanalysis and Neurobiology

By Morton F. Reiser. New York: Basic Books. 1984. Pp. 228.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein
Langley Porter Institute
401 Parnassus Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94143

Robert Wallerstein  

Starting with Freud, there always have been (some) analysts much concerned with the fit of psychoanalysis, as a natural science of the functioning of the mind, into the whole body of the biological natural sciences concerned with the functioning of the brain as the bodily organ providing the organic substrate of mental functions and with the body within which the brain plays out its co-ordinating and executive roles. As is so well known, Freud abandoned the quest for a neurologically grounded and explained mental apparatus out of the clear deficiencies of the neural science knowledge of his day though he never abandoned his conviction that the discovery of the precise chemical (hormonal?) carriers of the instinctual mental energies lay just around the corner, nor—some would assert—his continued reliance, through the hidden structure of his metapsychology, upon the very natural science assumptions and the model-building derived from them, that had powered the original more explicit efforts of his 'Project for a Scientific Psychology' (1895). (See in, this connexion, the book by Pribram & Gill, 1976, ) (and that by Sulloway, 1979.)

Morton Reiser is among the small group of analysts who are today trying to follow through on the belief that the time is now ripe, in terms of the matured neurobiology of the 1980's, for a fresh look at the possibilities for a true convergence of contemporary clinical psychoanalysis and the new neurobiology. His is not a philosophical effort. All the time-honoured and longstanding philosophical questions of the nature of the mind-brain or mind-body relationship have been simply bypassed, or better yet, taken for granted. In Reiser's one philosophy of science aside (in a footnote on page 16), he simply states: 'For the dialectical materialist (at least my sort of dialectical materialism) the firing of hypothalamic cells is anger; that is, "anger" and the "firing of particular cells" are statements that describe the same phenomenon at different hierarchical levels of discourse'.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: The Vital Issues, Vol I: Psychoanalysis as an Intellectual Discipline

Edited by John E. Gedo and George H. Pollock. New York: International Universities Press. 1984. Pp. 446.

Gordon Warme

Gordon Warme

Gordon E. Warme
Psychotherapy Center, Clarke Institute of Psychotherapy
250 College Street, Toronto M5T 1R8, Canada

Gordon Warme  

In an era of theoretical controversy, psychoanalytic theoreticians must pay increasing attention to issues in the philosophy of science. Since most psychoanalysts are preoccupied by clinical issues, philosophical niceties may seem irrelevant, but, just as quantum and nuclear physicists must grapple with how best to conceptualize the world, so psychoanalysts must grapple with how best to conceptualize the mind and its workings. The authors in this volume, although presenting us with a rich array of clinical examples, also face methodological issues head on. Facing these issues can only result in worthwhile theoretical developments.

Freud, with his typical ironic wit, often poked fun at professional philosophers for refusing to recognize his dramatic psychological insights. His rejection of philosophers must, however, be kept distinct from his attitude toward philosophy since, despite his mocking comments, Freud himself was guided by profoundly consistent philosophical principles. For him, all psychological discoveries were to be explained from a particular methodological position, namely, from a biological, natural science point of view. Although Freud's Lamarckianism and his drive theories are nowadays eschewed by many, his recognition that philosophical consistency is necessary for the development of any reliable theory remains as a model for us all. The body of Freud's theory was and is a remarkable achievement, precisely because he was able to render a world of psychological meanings in a credible biological framework. Adventitiously, the biological rendering of the theory made psychoanalytic notions acceptable in the greater scientific community and allowed them access to, especially, the medical-psychiatric establishment. Had Freud rendered his theory in terms of a quasi-literary textual critique, that is, as a hermeneutic discipline, it is unlikely that its acceptability would have been nearly as great.

This book is of interest because, again and again potent arguments are presented in one chapter, critically examined in another, and counter-arguments put forward in a third. The juxtaposition of serious theoretical differences serves the valuable function of undermining one's naive biases and should promote, in the reader, more discerning theoretical thinking. Surely such an outcome is exactly what the editors had in mind when they decided to publish the proceedings of the conference sponsored by the Chicago Psychoanalytic Society and the Institute of Psychoanalysis in 1981, of which this volume is a partial compilation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Repetition. Its Relationship to the Depressive Position

Hans Thorner

Hans Thorner

Hans A. Thorner
54 Clifton Hill
St John's Wood
London NW8

Hans Thorner  

SUMMARY

Thinking is painful as it progresses. Something of what is being achieved has to be left behind. The loss is being compensated by repetition in variations, as if the unconscious tries to restore what has been left behind. Looking back on the steps of thinking, one gains a feeling of security in one's conclusions if these steps are restored.

responseSet.title: Identification in the Theory and Technique of Psychoanalysis. Some Thoughts on its Farther Reaches and Functions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Identification in the Theory and Technique of Psychoanalysis. Some Thoughts on its Farther Reaches and Functions

Erich Simenauer

Erich Simenauer

Erich Simenauer
Bartningallee 16
1000 Berlin 21

Erich Simenauer  

SUMMARY

A re-valuation of the concept of identification appears pertinent as it has come to embrace a multitude of psychoanalytic notions. Historically Freud has already differentiated meaning and function of identification. The original choice of object in the infant is coupled with his narcissism; identification is unconscious, partial and ambivalent; both determine the outcome if object loss occurs. Through identification the individual is being constituted.

This notion culminated in Klein's concept of projective identification, among her other metapsychological extensions. By projecting not only phantasies and impulses but also part of its self the infant becomes capable of understanding and using symbols. Klein's notions on thought processes are commented upon here, with the inclusion of some language research, and it is argued that they are in line with Freud's paper 'Negation'.

The traditional notion of identification is then extended to an 'autoplastic' one which discusses autochthonic intrapersonal processes and hereditary factors. Autoplastic identification is primary, it becomes modified by the traditionally accepted mechanism of (alloplastic) identification.

A vignette from an analysis is given for illustration. The paper concludes with a discussion of the death-instinct in so far as it touches upon identification.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ambiguity of Change. An Inquiry Into the Nature of Psychoanalytic Reality

By Edgar Levenson. New York: Basic Books. 1983. Pp. 180

R. Dickinson

R. Dickinson

R. Hugh Dickinson
2271 Northeast Fifty-First Street, Seattle
Washington 98105

R. Dickinson  

Dr Levenson is an accomplished writer whose erudition is apparent throughout this work. His familiarity with our common and uncommon language is impressive and the reader may be well advised to have a dictionary at hand. His knowledge of linguistics and communications theory is obvious and his references to writers in these and other fields as well as psychoanalysis are numerous. The artistic writing itself tends to be persuasive and thereby may too readily lead some to agreement with his opinions. The work is indeed thought provoking and does cause a thinking person to reconsider his own position and tenets regarding theories of psychological therapy.

His thesis is that Freudian psychoanalysis is based on a mechanistic drive theory (libido and aggression) which has become outmoded. The interpersonal therapies of H.S. Sullivan et al., he says, moved beyond Freudian psychoanalysis, although they still contained a basic drive theory—namely the drive to contain and control a primary anxiety. The object relations theories of Klein, Winnicott, and others hoped to bridge the difference between the Freudian and the interpersonal theories, but they still retained the drive mechanism, an object-seeking drive. Levenson believes that communications theory related to the interpersonal principles does not need an energic drive theory. It seems to me, however, that he implicates a drive to communicate. Levenson's thesis, then, is summed up in two points: (1) that all analysts tap into the same linguistic process regardless of their hermeneutics or explanatory systems and (2) that a radical split has occurred in the utilization of the psychoanalytic method. It is his intention in this work to make a strong case for the 'superior relevance of the interpersonal perspective'. But with respectable humility, he realizes that this 'very considerable bone of contention' will not be buried by his efforts. In the making of his case, he tends towards extremes in describing the Freudian analyst on the one hand and the interpersonal analyst on the other. He recognizes that his use of sweeping generalizations to clarify his position will be irritating to some. He makes it appear, for instance, that the traditional psychoanalysts disregard or minimize the fact that they and their patients are 'persons' in the psychoanalytic situation, whereas the interpersonalists, he says, use their personalities in their therapeutic work with great effect.

responseSet.title: Imre Hermann (1889–1984)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imre Hermann (1889–1984)

György Vikár

György Vikár

György Vikár  

Imre Hermann, the grand old man of the Hungarian psychoanalytic movement, died last year on 22 February. Beside Sandor Ferenczi and Michael Balint he was the chief representative of the so called 'Budapest School of Psychoanalysis'. His main achievement was to ensure that psychoanalysis preserved its continuity in Hungary.

Hermann was a member of the IPA from 1919. His quiet and modest personality showed strength and courage which enabled him to persist in his views during great historical changes. He built his oeuvre unhesitatingly, helped by his wife, Alice Hermann-Cziner—herself an analyst. She stood beside him for more than 50 years as a friend and collaborator; they wrote a number of publications jointly.

Hermann graduated from the Medical School of Budapest University and began as a specialist in mental disorders. In his university years he had already taken an interest in psychology. He was a student of Géza Révész, who was later to achieve world fame as a master of experimental psychology and skill assessment. Around this time Hermann attended one of Sándor Ferenczi's lectures on psychoanalysis. He soon became a passionate follower of the new science and a student of Ferenczi. He was accompanied throughout his activity, by the spirit of impartial scientific research.

Hermann perpetually extended the scope of psychoanalysis to include newer fields. His first major works approach the psychology of thinking from the analytical angle: Psychoanalyse und Logik (1924); Das Ich und das Denken (1929). In a series of experiments, published in 1921, he examined the formal laws of choice. He came to the conclusion that when a choice is to be made from a series of uniform elements, e.g. coins, matches, a tendency to marginal choice first prevails and it is around the age of 6 that the tendency of medium choice, characteristic of adults, appears. In regressive conditions, in dreams, in certain psychoses, etc. the tendency to marginal choice returns. A philosophical approach characterizes his book entitled Psychoanalysis as a Method (1963), Psychoanalyse als Methode (1933), which is still a standard textbook in Hungarian psychoanalytic training.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Early Development and Education of the Child

By W. Hoffer. Edited by M. Brierley. New York: Jason Aronson and London: Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. 1981. Pp. 220.

Lester Isenstadt

Lester Isenstadt

Lester Isenstadt
2702 Dana Street, Berkeley, CA 94705

Lester Isenstadt  

Willi Hoffer's psychoanalytic career has its origins in the psychological study of children and this interest persisted throughout and was coextensive with his general psychoanalytic career. Hoffer's contributions span two phases in the history of psychoanalytic child psychology located by Ernst Kris around a chronological dividing line in the early 1920's. Early Development and Education of the Child is a tribute to Willi Hoffer and his scientific work in both phases of the psychoanalytic study of child development. The focus in the first phase was on the typical conflicts and danger situations and their relation to the maturational sequence of libidinal development. The second phase was a response to the development of ego psychology and it is characterized by the effort to integrate a wider set of factors in the psychoanalytic account of personality development.

Part I of the volume consists of papers written between 1949 and 1966, an era in psychoanalysis when analysts were responding to exhortations for the more complete study of the full scope of ego ontogenesis. In 1945 Hartmann and Kris had looked back over progress in the theory of ego development during the previous two decades and concluded that genetic propositions about ego development were still incomplete and unsatisfactory. Freud's fundamental developmental propositions implied by and dispersed within his discussions of the various metapsychological perspectives had led to more knowledge about the development of the libido and superego than about the ego. They encouraged a widening of the data base. Retrospective methods within the analytic setting could not reconstruct the interplay of maturational and environmental factors which co-determine the development of those ego functions needed to synthesize tensions and reduce conflict and anxiety. In 1945 Hartmann and Kris recommended the constant integration of two observational methods, psychoanalysis and direct observation techniques, in order to integrate data from different sources and fill out formulations concerning ego development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Structural Hypothesis: An Evolutionary Perspective

By Arnold Rothstein. New York: International Universities Press. 1983. Pp. 194.

Jacob Jacobson

Jacob Jacobson

Jacob G. Jacobson
1636 16th St. Boulder, Colorado 80302

Jacob Jacobson  

As the subtitle promises, Rothstein undertakes in this book the worthy enterprise of reviewing psychoanalytic structural theory as an evolving organic theory, contributing along the way to its ongoing expansion in response to new experience and new data. He contrasts this with what he calls the revolutionary effort which view structural theory as a finite finished product, now outmoded, beyond repair, and needing to be discarded and replaced. He expands here what he has written previously to elucidate his view of structural theory as a conceptual framework adequate to encompass primitive, later preoedipal, and oedipal psychopathologies in a clinically meaningful and useful way.

Against a background of Thomas Kuhn's views of the paradigm in science, the author begins with a review of the effects of irrational narcissistic investments on our theories, on theory-building, and on the retention or discarding of particular theoretical frameworks. A narcissistic investment in a given paradigm, the traditional or the new, holds forth the promise, or conviction, of perfection. Internalized into the scientist's self-representation, it arms him against helplessness and uncertainty. Rothstein presents an interesting elaboration of certain special issues in psychoanalysis which he believes contribute to the pressures toward making paradigm changes. These include frustration over limits to the therapeutic effectiveness of psychoanalysis, which stimulates modifications in search of more favourable result with a broader spectrum of patients, and controversy over such issues as the primacy of oedipal vs pre-oedipal conflicts. He presents interesting discussions of the contributions of Mahler and Kohut, describing the efforts of the one to remain within the conceptual umbrella of traditional metapsychology, and of the other to emphasize, in latter years at least, the radical nature of his departures. These efforts to remain or depart are not necessarily proportional to the degree of novelty of the hypotheses or of the shifts of emphasis being presented. Rothstein illustrates through this the impact of personality variables, over and above scientific considerations, which inevitably enter into questions of whether an existing paradigm is to be discarded, or simply modified, in the face of new and conflicting data.

responseSet.title: Understanding Matte-Blanco
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Matte-Blanco

Ross Skelton

Ross Skelton

Ross Skelton
Philosophy Department Trinity College
Dublin 2
Eire

Ross Skelton  

DEAR SIR,

In a very thorough reply to my paper 'Understanding Matte-Blanco' (this journal 1984,

(March 1985)

504

65) which is found in the same volume, Matte-Blanco seems not to have understood my single, though I believe crucial, criticism of Bi-logic, namely that it is inconsistent. He is under the impression that because the two basic principles (Generalisation and Symmetry) do not always contradict, or only contradict in some specific aspect, his system remains intact. I would like to correct this impression.

Matte-Blanco teaches us that the Unconscious does in some measure use two valued logic and that the principle of Generalisation (Predicate thinking) represents this. But the 'deeper' the Unconscious manifestation the less respect for the two valued logic and Freud has listed at least four ways the Unconscious proper violates the straight and narrow path of conventional logical thinking. These violations are largely explained by his Principle of Symmetry (relations are reversible). Bi-logic, the logic of the Unconscious is the intertwining of these two principles.

I have no quarrel with this imaginative perspective on the Primary and Secondary processes of the mind and when Matte-Blanco proposes to allow that at some times these principles contradict and at other times they do not, we can agree that in his perspective this is how things are in the Unconscious. However, from a strictly logical point of view, for a logic to be a valid logic (i.e. never to lead us from true statements to false ones) we require that its basic principles should never contradict. This is where he makes a serious error of logic when he says:

It is no wonder, then, that the principle of Symmetry, which describes the violations of bivalent logic, should at times—not always be in contradiction with the principle of Generalisation—a truism (p. 457)

and again:

In other words when the principle of Symmetry holds, the principle of contradiction is not always violated (p. 457)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Repression' or Sea-Change—Fenichel's Rundbriefe and the 'Political Analysts' of the 1930's

Sanford Gifford

Sanford Gifford

Sanford Gifford
Brigham and Women's Hospital
75 Francis Street
Boston
MA 02115

Sanford Gifford  

This is primarily a review of Russell Jacoby's recent book (1983), which provides fascinating glimpses of Fenichel's unpublished Rundbriefe, an amazing correspondence conducted almost single-handedly from 1932 to 1945. Despite its unfortunate title, The Repression of Psychoanalysis, this short, scholarly but highly readable book has many virtues, in reminding us of a more glorious past, as well as some debatable interpretations of changes in the analytic movement since the Second World War. Jacoby recalls our European forebears at a time when their numbers have dwindled and a new generation of analysts is too young to have known them as teachers. Above all he reminds us of a youthful, revolutionary era in psychoanalysis when the movement was small and its strongest appeal was to intellectual rebels of all kinds. First Jacoby chronicles this golden age of analysis in the Vienna and Berlin of the 1920s and '30s, in broad strokes but with an impressive command of cultural and historical detail, much of it unfamiliar to young American analysts. Then he comes to the principal theme of his book, the flight of German-speaking analysts from Nazi Europe and the complex transformation of analysis in this country that Stuart Hughes has called The Sea-Change(1975). Whether one welcomes or deplores this seemingly inevitable metamorphosis, in which a heady European wine was diluted into a mass-produced American blend, the process contained many contradictory elements. Jacoby's most valuable contribution is his use of Fenichel, and the small circle of 'political analysts' who shared his Rundbriefe, as exemplary figures in illustrating the Americanization and embourgeoisement of analysis.

In the middle two-thirds of the book, Jacoby gives vivid excerpts and concise summaries from his unique material, the Rundbriefe that Fenichel intended to be an exchange of letters among his six closest colleagues but which became, over eleven and a half years, a kind of one-man newsletter, with reviews of meetings, papers, books and current events in psychoanalysis. There are 119 so-called 'letters', filling over 3000 typed pages, which cover the period from Fenichel's flight from Berlin in 1932 to 1945, shortly before his death. Jacoby has mastered this gargantuan document, assembled from scattered sources, and he has annotated its background with a wide-ranging knowledge of scholarly sources. Resisting, I presume, the temptation to edit the entire correspondence, he quotes short, pertinent excerpts in his own translation, and whets our appetite for more.

[[RunningHead]]

'The Future of Psychoanalysis'

George Allison M.D.

George Allison

George H. Allison, M.D.
2271 Northeast Fifty-First Street
Seattle
Washington 98105

George Allison  

DEAR DR HAYLEY,

In response to Evelyne Schwaber's letter to you of 12 May, 1985 regarding my review of The Future of Psychoanalysis. Essays in Honor of Heinz Kohut, edited by A. Goldberg (1983), to which she contributed the article 'Construction, Reconstruction, and the Mode of Clinical Attunement', I must agree with her criticism in the main. Because her contribution was grouped in the section on Dreams, Technique, and Clinical Theory with three other 'committed self-psychologists' (as I see them) I erred in so classifying Dr Schwaber. Certainly her self identity in this regard is critical and decisive. Nevertheless, some of her readers, besides myself, may have fallen into the same error, according to discussions I have heard locally and elsewhere. It is regrettable therefore, that this and her other papers with which I am familiar in the same area of clinical attunement and mode of analytic listening may be mistakenly taken to constitute a basis for the judgment that she derives her thesis from Kohut's later writings as well as his earlier ones. Similarly, she has good reason to dissent from my conceptualization that her 'mode of attunement' is similar, parallel to, or equivalent to 'total empathic immersion'. This, however, was not a (mis)quote from her paper but a judgment that her concept fits into the same conceptual framework—erroneous or mistaken as this view may be, compared to her own meaning. Her careful letter to you serves to explicate her position usefully, and I am glad that the review afforded her the opportunity and the forum for this clarification.

I wish to point out (as acknowledged by Dr Schwaber) that I was attempting to discuss a group of contributions together, as organized by the editor (not by me). Total empathic immersion, self states and transmuting internalizations were erroneously applied or implied as pertaining to her contribution. These terms should have been limited to three others in the group. I was attempting to integrate and connect all four to Kohut's contribution in the same way. The comment about the 'grey area' as to what type of psychopathology this group of authors had in mind would also not pertain since she reaffirms 'no intention to illustrate clinical issues representative of any particular kinds of psychopathology'.

responseSet.title: The Late Dr Imre Hermann
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Late Dr Imre Hermann

Otto Thaler M.D.

Otto Thaler

Otto F. Thaler, M.D., President
The Western New York Psychoanalytic Association
 Department of Psychiatry University of Rochester Medical Center
300 Crittenden Boulevard
Rochester
New York 14642

Otto Thaler  

DEAR DR HAYLEY,

I was saddened to read of the death of Dr Imre Hermann in the last number of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. The news of Dr Hermann's death brought back memories of my association with him during several years in the late 1960's and early '70's. It was during these years that I carried on a frequent and quite voluminous correspondence with Dr Hermann. Through Dr Hermann's friend and colleague, Dr Sandor Feldman, who himself died twelve years ago, I was persuaded to prepare a translation of Dr Hermann's monograph 'Method in Psychoanalysis', from German into English. Dr Hermann provided me with a stream of revisions and emendations of that work, first published in German in 1933 and since, in a second edition, also in German in 1963. Although I completed my translation, including Dr Hermann's corrections, I could not get any of our psychoanalytic publishing houses to accept it and finally gave up the attempt. I do not believe it was ever published in English, though the obituary seems to indicate that. The second edition was a German language version. I did correspond with Dr Sutherland about this matter during his tenure as editor, pointing out to him that a very favourable review of the second edition of 'method' contained the statement that Dr Hermann's work was not translatable but that I had indeed a translation at hand. Even though Dr Sutherland expressed interest and reviewed the manuscript of my translation, nothing ever came of it.

I met Dr Hermann on one occasion about fifteen years ago during I believe, his first and only visit to the United States. Dr Feldman asked me at that time to join him and a few other colleagues in New York to meet Dr Hermann. As a footnote to the history of psychoanalysis, it is worthwhile to put on record belatedly the occurrence of the meeting and those present: Dr Robert Bak, Dr Sandor Lorand, Dr Andrew Peto, Dr Sandor Feldman, Dr Otto Thaler—all meeting with Dr Imre Hermann. It was a warm and joyous occasion. I recall that everyone attempted to speak German for my benefit, since Dr Hermann spoke no English, and I no Hungarian. It was a valiant attempt on the part of all; but as is inevitable when Hungarians meet, soon everyone lapsed into Hungarian, leaving me free to observe the happy reunion without the benefit of understanding what was said. I came away with an impression Dr Hermann as a kindly, gentle man with a charming sparkle and a courtly manner. I cherish his memory, the breadth of his scholarship and his extraordinary dedication to psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and the Mind

By Ilham Dilman. London: Basil Blackwell. 1984. Pp. 216.

Neville Symington

Neville Symington

Neville Symington
20 Burgess Hill, London NW2 2DA

Neville Symington  

A man who has an unconscious wish to hurt can never know it.

If the wish becomes conscious it is a different man who knows it.

Nowhere in his book does Ilham Dilman make this exact statement but a central concern of this work is to establish that a change in consciousness necessarily means a change in the identity of the subject and he demonstrates convincingly that this was Freud's own understanding though it is often obscured by his mechanistic and causal language. Dilman says,

In his Introductory Lectures, speaking about the 'inner change' which psycho-analytic therapy aims at, Freud says: 'A neurotic who has been cured has really became a different person, although at bottom of course he remains the same—this is, he has become his best self, what he would have been under the most favourable conditions'. He says that the analyst aims at bringing about such a change by 'making conscious the unconscious, removing repressions, filling in the gaps in memory'.

Freud does not equate this with self-knowledge. We have seen that the becoming conscious of what is unconscious is a change in the person. But the 'inner change' which Freud speaks of in the above passage goes beyond that. It is a change that involves the 'growth of the self'. Hence Freud's reference to 'what he (the neurotic) would have been under the most favourable conditions'—that is conditions which would not have thwarted or arrested his development.

When the ego engages in forcing away aspects of the self and thereby preventing development we have repression. And here we are confronted with the mysterious fact that there is in human beings such a strong force going counter to growth.

Dilman explicates repression in this way but does not explore the reasons for its existence. I was not sure whether he attributed the repressive force to the death instinct as Freud understood it. However, this in no way detracts from the clarity with which he explains this cornerstone of analytic theory. What emerges as the book proceeds is a clear picture of psychological development as a process of integration so that there is an expansion of the self. Repression is then seen as the force in the personality that tries to prevent this process. What psychoanalytical therapy aims at is an autonomy of the ego. It is a goal that the ego should become master of its own house through integration of all the available parts of the self and through assimilation of the superego by the ego.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toward Bion's Starting Point: Between Catastrophe and Faith

Michael Eigen

Michael Eigen

Michael Eigen
225 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Michael Eigen  

SUMMARY

Catastrophe and faith are explored as the most basic terms of Bion's work. In doing so it is shown how Bion gets underneath Kleinian concepts to expand the depth phenomenology of a catastrophic sense of origins. For Bion the self is born, evolves and dissolves with a sense of catastrophe. Whether or not Bion is right in various aspects of his account, catastrophe is an elemental term of our existence. For Bion, Faith is a proper primordial and developed response to catastrophe. This paper discusses aspects of the relationship between faith, knowledge, container-contained and catastrophe.

[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Organization, Projective Identification, and the Formation of the Identificate

Leslie Sohn

Leslie Sohn

Leslie Sohn
11 Fitzroy Road
London NW1

Leslie Sohn  

SUMMARY

Clinical examples are given, varying from what would appear to be self-sufficient normal people, to overtly psychotic cases, in order to illustrate the development of a narcissistic organization of varying intensity and permanence.

That is to say, an identification via projective identification has taken place, which heightens intrinsic omnipotence, to allow what has been termed the identificate to believe that it has

212

become the desired object—and thereby that within this spuriously organized ego-structure exist the characteristics and functions of the object or part object that has been taken over. Varying forms of such identificatory process are illustrated. The functions of these 'successful defence' manoeuvres are to obviate any feelings of an awareness of envy, although they may be overtly envious attacks within themselves, secondly they nullify any awareness of dependence, and also nullify awareness of need and illness, and thirdly they maintain the narcissistic organization by producing a successful identificate.

Phantasy Effects that which it Represents

Neville Symington

Neville Symington

Neville Symington
20 Burgess Hill
London NW2 2DA

Neville Symington  

SUMMARY

This paper sets out to show that phantasy does not just inhabit a mental realm within the individual. With the help of some clinical examples, it tries to demonstrate that phantasy is itself

356

an active agent, so that the image within the phantasy is brought into effect through a subtle stimulation of the social environment. The paper ends by suggesting the way in which this can happen.

[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Problems in Analysts

Janet Finell

Janet Finell

Janet Schumacher Finell
41 Fifth Avenue
New York
NY 10003

Janet Finell  

SUMMARY

Narcissistic problems in analysts reflect the same dynamics as they do in patients. Splitting, projection, denial, and ego fragmentation defend against the experiencing of painful affects. Idealization and grandiosity, aggression, power, and exhibitionism and their opposites are the essential issues in narcissism. Psychoanalytic work can be extremely gratifying to the analyst's narcissism, leaving little incentive to resolve it personally or in one's patients. Politics and other extra-analytic situations can become the repository of split-off hostile and devaluing feelings while the personal analyst remains idealized.

Narcissism in analysts is relatively unexplored in the vast literature on countertransference, indicating that it arouses shameful and secretive reactions. Case material illustrated the points made. The overall conclusion is that the problem is a difficult one, particularly when the transference involves symbiotic features and mutual gratification. Working through narcissism should be central in the analyst's personal analysis in order to foster high levels of ego integration, self development, and analytic ability.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Developmental Approach to Narcissism: A Concise Review

David Smith

David Smith

David L. Smith
8 Brenchley Gardens
Forest Hill
London SE23 3QS

David Smith  

SUMMARY

Freud's thinking regarding the role of narcissism in infantile development evolved through three distinct stages. During the first period (1909–1914) he described a sequence of three stages: auto-erotism, narcissism and object-love. In one work during this period he divided the third stage into a stage of homosexual object-choice followed by a stage of heterosexual object-choice. During the second period (1915–1923) he abandoned the idea of a stage of auto-erotism and eventually described a stage of primary narcissism—referred to as the stage of the original reality ego—followed by the stage of the purified pleasure ego and a semi-narcissistic anal-sadistic stage. This re-orientation involved a re-definition of narcissism in terms of illusions of self-sufficiency. During the third period (1923–1939) Freud considerably expanded his view on the

495

narcissistic dynamics of the oral phase in terms of primary identification, defensive secondary identification and finally relinquishment of the breast as a part of the self. The phallic phase was also added. Any discussion of Freud's theory of narcissism must take the evolution of his position into account.

Narcissism: Its Structures, Systems and Affects

Alan Parkin

Alan Parkin

Alan Parkin
276 St. Clair Avenue West
Toronto
Ontario M4V 1R9

Alan Parkin  

SUMMARY

The growth of object relationships may be studied along either the developmental line of the

153

discharge-object or that of the reflexive-object. The former is the well-known line of development from the need-satisfying object to the constant object and is a study of id-ego relationships. The developmental line of the reflexive-object, on the other hand, follows the history of the introjects and is a study of either ego-superego or ego-ego ideal relationships. It is the latter which constitutes the study of narcissism.

The development of the ideal ego out of the ego nuclei leads eventually to the child's struggle to maintain a sense of omnipotence and to the narcissistic crisis. Out of this struggle the introjection of the idealized mother occurs which, as the ego ideal, continues the systemic line of development of narcissism.

Various narcissistic states and affects such as shame, humiliation, depression, grandiosity, pomposity, arrogance, adoration and enthralment are the result of either hypercathexis of the introjects of the ego ideal (or of persisting remnants of the ideal ego) and the resulting conflict with the ego, or hypocathexis of the introjects and resulting dormancy of the system ego ideal.

[[RunningHead]]

'Everybody Must be Just Like Me': Observations on Female Castration Anxiety

Elizabeth Mayer

Elizabeth Mayer

Elizabeth Lloyd Mayer
2435 Russell Street
Berkeley, CA 94705

Elizabeth Mayer  

SUMMARY

The concept of primary femininity entails an assumption that the girl develops some mental representation of genital femaleness at an early age. I have suggested one form that such a mental representation may take and a particular expression of female castration anxiety that may develop in consequence. I have suggested that the young girl begins by assuming that everyone has a vulva like hers, with the possibility of an opening and the possibility of an inside space. Among the consequences of such an assumption may, in certain girls, be the development of a castration fantasy in which males represent the frightening possibility that such an opening in a female could be endangered, lost, or closed up as that opening is imagined to be in males. I have suggested that certain sterotypic features of the ways in which some women characterize men (i.e. men are emotionally closed, unable to be receptive, without access to inner feelings or sensitivities, etc.) may be understood at least in part as derivatives of this fantasy.

Transference: The Total Situation

Betty Joseph

Betty Joseph

Betty Joseph
36 Clifton Hill
London NW8 0QG

Betty Joseph  

SUMMARY

I have tried in this paper to discuss how I think we are tending to use the concept of transference today. I have stressed the importance of seeing transference as a living relationship in which there is constant movement and change. I have indicated how everything of importance in the patient's psychic organization based on his early and habitual ways of functioning, his fantasies, impulses, defences and conflicts, will be lived out in some way in the transference. In addition, everything that the analyst is or says is likely to be responded to according to the patient's own psychic make-up, rather than the analyst's intentions and the meaning he gives to his interpretations. I have thus tried to discuss how the way in which our patients communicate their problems to us is frequently beyond their individual

453

associations and beyond their words, and can often only be gauged by means of the countertransference. These are some of the points that I think we need to consider under the rubric of the total situations which are transferred from the past.

Cruelty and Narrowmindedness

Eric Brenman

Eric Brenman

Eric Brenman
7 Regents Park Terrace
London NW1 7EE

Eric Brenman  

SUMMARY

The contention of this paper is that human understanding modifies cruelty, and that in order that cruelty can remain unmodified various mechanisms are employed. The most important processes include the worship of omnipotence which is felt to be superior to human love and forgiveness, the clinging to omnipotence as a defence against depression, and the sanctification of grievance and revenge. In order to avoid conscious guilt, the perceptions of the mind are narrowed to give ostensible justification to the cruelty, and the obviation of redeeming features in the object.

The paper explores how these processes operate and how by virtue of projection the analyst's interpretations are perceived as cruel, and how the patient arranges to be locked in this vicious circle.

Technical problems of dealing with these forces are explored together with the task of bringing alive the human concern that modifies cruelty.

responseSet.title: On the Question of Revision of the Standard Edition of Freud's Writing
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Question of Revision of the Standard Edition of Freud's Writing

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines  

The Strachey Standard Edition of Freud's works, published between 1955 and 1967, was then, and still is, a great scientific and literary achievement. The replacement of earlier diverse translations which often misrepresented and distorted Freud's writings, which conflicted with each other and confused the reader, by a uniform edition where the work of translation was shared between a small, highly skilled, knowledgeable team of psychoanalysts who devoted a massive amount of attention to issues of terminology and to the clarification of concepts, was greeted with almost universal praise, admiration and gratitude. It was a great psychoanalytic publication, a considerable financial risk.

Generations of psychoanalysts have been raised on Strachey, have been educated by his great editorial achievements in which he traced the historical development of Freud's concepts and clarified the essence of his constant revisions of his theories in the light of further psychoanalytic experience, theoretical sophistication and in response to criticism of his ideas. Analysts have appreciated the magisterial quality of the writing, appreciated the cool clarity of style of a writer who had been nourished on the European tradition of classical translation. Few then questioned whether Strachey's translation represented as authentic a version of Freud's ideas as possible. The authority of Anna Freud reinforced this almost universal acceptance.

What then has happened since then, especially in the last decade, that Strachey's version has begun seriously to be questioned and challenged? Several factors have to be taken into account. It is inevitable that the achievements of one generation will be challenged by the next. This is healthy and demonstrates the liveliness and evolution of psychoanalytic thought. Nowadays, non-German speaking analysts are learning German so as to be able to read Freud in the original and to obtain their own views of his ideas. They appreciate Freud's mastery of German style, the subtleties and achievements of which have recently been presented to us by the Canadian analyst and literary scholar, Patrick Mahony (1982). Others, on the basis of a close examination of Strachey's work have argued that Strachey's Freud is not the authentic Freud, that Strachey subtly and powerfully emended Freud's ideas so that eventually they presented a far greater uniformity than Freud himself had intended. It has been seriously argued, for instance, that what we accept as the Structural Theory is more Strachey's invention than Freud's! (Ornston, 1982.) If so, the consequences of this argument are far reaching indeed, as generations of analysts have been raised to regard the Structural Theory as a great achievement of Freud's model building, the foundation of much later theoretical superstructure. Opposition to the Structural Model, arguments that it was mechanistic and over-schematic, too far removed from the facts of human development and from clinical experience, led to radical revisions such as Fairbairn's emphasis on the primacy of Object Relations and to Kohut's on the primary significance of self-experience. But if Freud never ceased to describe the experience of the Self, did not completely substitute the conceptually abstract Ego for the experience-close Self, do Self psychologists and Object Relations theorists need to diverge so radically from Freud? Do we need the two paradigms of Guilty Man and Tragic Man?

[[RunningHead]]

On Transference Manifestations in Male Patients with Female Analysts

Marianne Goldberger and Dorothy Evans

Dorothy Evans

Marianne Goldberger
4601 Fieldston Road
Riverdale, New York 10471
Dorothy Evans
NEW YORK

Dorothy Evans  

SUMMARY

The transference issues that are specific to the psychoanalysis of male patients by female analysts have not been widely discussed in the literature. Most of the literature on this subject has indicated, contrary to the findings reported here, that full and intense erotic transference manifestations are rarely if ever seen in this therapeutic dyad. In order to clarify the issues, we have offered some definitions that may serve as a framework for future discussions.

We have utilized clinical examples from the psychoanalyses of five patients to illustrate certain aspects of the transference. We have demonstrated that male patients display a full range of erotic transference phenomena with female analysts and have focused attention on certain special features of these transferences. For example, in some cases it was only after considerable analysis of defences against aggression that the erotic transference manifestations emerged fully. We have also shown that paternal transferences occur regularly, though they may be difficult to elucidate until the later stages of analysis. We have paid special attention to the feared loss of maleness that attends male patients' identification with their female analysts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Female Analyst and the Erotized Transference

Eva Lester

Eva Lester

Eva P. Lester
Child and Adolescent Service Allan Memorial Institute McGill University
3666 McTavish Street
Montreal PQ H3A 1Y2

Eva Lester  

SUMMARY

The concept of erotized transference is discussed from Freud's original writings on the subject to those of recent contributors. It is noted that there exists an almost complete absence of reports in the literature of male patients developing an erotized transference to their female analysts. The question of how significant the gender of the analyst is in the unfolding of the transference and its vicissitudes is raised. It is maintained that the four possible analyst/analysand dyads differ qualitatively in that resistance and identification in the transference may be enhanced or inhibited by the gender of the analyst and/or the homosexual or heterosexual nature of the dyad. The erotized transference to the female analyst is focused upon as a vehicle to the study of such qualitative differences. It is hypothesized that the expression of strong erotic urges to the female analyst by the male patient is somewhat inhibited by the fantasy of the overwhelming pre-oedipal mother. In contrast, such erotic feelings are freely expressed by the female patient. Two cases, a male and a female, are described to support the above hypothesis.

[[RunningHead]]

On Potential Space

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

Thomas H. Ogden
1721 Scott Street
San Francisco
CA 94115

Thomas Ogden  

SUMMARY

In this paper, I have proposed that Winnicott's concept of potential space might be understood as a state of mind based upon a series of dialectical relationships between fantasy and reality, me and not-me, symbol and symbolized, etc., each pole of the dialectic creating, informing, and negating the other. The achievement of such a dialectical process occurs by means of a developmental advance from the 'invisible oneness' of the mother-infant unit to the subjective 'three-ness' of the mother-and-infant (as symbolic objects) and the infant (as interpreting subject). Failure to create or maintain the dialectical process leads to specific forms of psychopathology that include the experience of the fantasy object as a thing in itself, the defensive use of reality that forecloses imagination, the relationship to a fetish object, and the state of 'non-experience'. The 'processing' of a projective identification is understood as the re-establishment of the recipient's capacity to maintain a dialectical process (e.g. of me and not-me) that had been limited in the course of the recipient's unconscious participation in the projector's externalized unconscious fantasy.

Hysteria

Eric Brenman

Eric Brenman

Eric Brenman
7 Regents Park Terrace
London NW1 7EE

Eric Brenman  

SUMMARY

This paper attempts to link the symptomatology in hysteria with the hysterical personality. The view put forward is that in hysteria the patient is governed by fears of catastrophe coupled with complete denial of problems: this can result in catastrophic symptoms together with belle indifference or a personality split between total catastrophe and the belief there are no problems.

The author shows the operation of these forces on the transference/countertransference on how the 'hysteric' is governed by an external object using the same devices. The focus of the paper deals with how the hysteric uses this constellation to avoid more serious breakdown (psychosis) and how it becomes a way of life.

[[RunningHead]]

Working Through in the Countertransference

Irma Pick

Irma Pick

Irma Brenman Pick
7 Regents Park Terrace
London NW1 7EE

Irma Pick  

SUMMARY

This paper is intended as a development of Strachey's classic paper 'The nature of the therapeutic action of psycho-analysis'. Strachey states that the full or deep transference experience is disturbing to the analyst; that which the analyst most fears and most wishes to avoid. He also stresses that conveying an interpretation in a calm way is necessary. The area addressed is the task of coping with these strong countertransference experiences and maintaining the analytic technique of interpretation. The clinical illustrations attempt to show something of the process of transformation or working through in the analyst, as well as showing that the patient is consciously or unconsciously mindful as to whether the analyst evades or meets the issues.

The contention that the analyst is not affected by these experiences is both false and would convey to the patient that his plight, pain and behaviour are emotionally ignored by the analyst. It is suggested that if we keep emotions out, we are in danger of keeping out the love which mitigates the hatred, allowing the so-called pursuit of truth to be governed by hatred. What appears as dispassionate may contain the murder of love and concern. How the analyst allows himself to have the experiences, work through and transform them into a useful interpretation is the issue studied in this paper.

responseSet.title: Nosography and Theory of the Schizophrenias
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Nosography and Theory of the Schizophrenias

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman
The Anna Freud Centre
London NW3

Thomas Freeman  

SUMMARY

A theory of the schizophrenias must take account of the varieties of symptomatology and courses of illness which occur. Neglect of nosography has led to controversy about object relations in the schizophrenias. At the onset of all schizophrenic psychoses real object relations are replaced by 'psychotic' identifications. The object is retained despite its regressed state. Absence of remission and courses to end states can be attributed to loss of the object. This leads to delusional object relations based on fantasy.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Theory and the Problem of Creativity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Theory and the Problem of Creativity

Giordano Fossi

Giordano Fossi

Giordano Fossi
Piazza Pitti 12
50125 Florence

Giordano Fossi  

SUMMARY

The current crisis of psychoanalysis also involves studies of various forms of creativity. After having pointed out the need for distinction between clinical-empirical theory and hypothetical theory (such as metapsychology) the author identifies and summarizes a number of trends of investigation as reported in our literature: libidinal-energetic, contenutistic (unconscious fantasies), anthropomorphic (of the ego), aggression-reparation, phenomenological, and object-relationship approaches. The role played by metapsychological concepts (the author, in agreement with those who consider them unacceptable, discusses some of the most well-known criticisms), and the confusion between theoretical levels are responsible for having made the psychoanalytical contribution entirely unsatisfactory at a higher explanatory level and for having hindered adequate reorganization of data of an empirical nature. After having examined several elements involved in creativity (symbolism, role of pathology and body experience, etc.), the author outlines a personal theoretical hypothesis of the structural type as a basis for the establishment of a clinical-empirical theory which, as regards research on creativity too, may constitute the chief field of investigation for psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Family Pathology and the Infantile Neurosis

Roy Calogeras and Toni Alston

Toni Alston

Roy C. Calogeras
Dept. of Psychiatry (Box 4) U.S. Army Hospital Frankfurt
Giessener Strasse
6 Frankfurt/M/
Toni M. Alston
Universität Mainz Beratungsstelle für studentische Lebensfragen
Postfach 2980
65 Mainz

Toni Alston  

SUMMARY

In this study we have attempted to investigate the connexion (in the sense of a complex interplay) between the infantile and the adult neurosis where severe family pathology has been an important determining force. In the clinical illustrations of two female patients we tried to determine the fate (i.e. in terms of the compulsion to repeat) of the early infantile conflicts and experiences within the purview of their pathological family condition. In sorting this out, we distinguished between those repetitions viewed as passive reproductions and those repetitions viewed as re-creative; the former finding their way essentially into the adult neurosis, the latter finding their way into parts of the personality under the aegis of their ego's organizing activity. The fashion in which the family pathology impinged upon the adult neurosis made the development of the transference in the analytic situation a most difficult arena for clarification, since there was a tendency to confuse past (early) events, past fantasy, current reality and current fantasy. In concluding we noted that both Oedipus complex and oedipal drama came together in these patients, revealing the link between the tragedy of their life-long family situation and what was termed a 'fate neurosis'.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Envy, the Womb and the Nature of Goodness—A Reappraisal of the Death Instinct

Neil Maizels

Neil Maizels

Neil Maizels
2/24 Shakespeare Grove
Hawthorn, 3122
Victoria
Australia

Neil Maizels  

SUMMARY

The Freudian and Kleinian conceptions of the struggle between 'life' and 'death' instincts are not identical. This paper puts forward a model which attempts both to reconcile and add to the differences between them, whilst making some suggestions about the 'nature of goodness'.

Attention is focused on the phantasy of returning to the womb and its consequent anxieties to the growing, active ego. Such anxiety, and its reverse, can only be moderated by the introjection of an object which is capable of creatively resolving the resulting conflicts. When envious feelings are not tolerated the impetus for such an introjection is reduced, which, in turn, increases the envy.

responseSet.title: How Does Self Psychology Differ in Practice?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Does Self Psychology Differ in Practice?

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein
Dept. of Psychiatry & Langley Porter Psychiatric Institute
401 Parnassus Avenue
San Francisco 94143

Robert Wallerstein  

SUMMARY

In his third and posthumous book, How Does Analysis Cure?, Kohut completes the clinical and theoretical system of self psychology as a self-contained psychoanalytic psychology, different in crucial ways from what he calls 'traditional analysis'. It is a psychology of the supraordinate self and its developmental struggle for cohesion, with a psychopathology and a therapy based on conceptions of deficit and of restoration rather than of conflict and its resolution. Within this framework many usually central psychoanalytic conceptualizations are diminished, the roles of drives and defences, the Oedipus complex as the organizing nodal point of character development, the nature of defence and resistance analysis, etc. Many psychoanalytic

402

critics of self psychology see it, in its clinical application, as but a species of (supportive) psychotherapy rather than proper psychoanalysis. My contention is rather that the same kinds of infiltrations by psychotherapeutic manoeuvres and interventions can be found in classical analysis as well, when subjected to the same intensive scrutiny. I therefore regard Kohut's self psychology not as a psychotherapy as compared with proper analysis, but rather as a new and variant theoretical school of psychoanalysis. I had hoped that the clinical insights of self psychology could have been integrated into the mainstream of classical psychoanalysis—as an enriching supplementation—rather than being directed off (Kohut's choice) into new theory formation and yet another separate theoretical perspective and 'school' within the corpus of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impaired Spatial Structures

Judit Székács

Judit Székács

Judit Székács
1137 Pozsonyi 38
Budapest XIII
Hungary

Judit Székács  

SUMMARY

Our knowledge of the very early processes of human development, such as how the body becomes experienced as a three-dimensional spatial structure containing different functional cavities that communicate with the outer world via bodily orifices which are regulated by appropriate sphincter control, is far from being complete.

Analysing spatial aspects of psychodynamic phenomena can bring us closer to understanding the nature of internal and external spaces and their role in harmonious and disturbed psychic development.

In the paper attention is called to special symbolic connexions between open and strictly closed spaces, malfunctioning boundaries in the outside world and insufficient-containing functions and respective disorders in the development of the ego and ego boundaries.

Specific defensive manoeuvres, aimed at annihilating inner spaces and bringing the organism to a 'thing-like', two-dimensional way of existence, are demonstrated in the material of patients with severe psychosomatic diseases and identity problems.

Eugene Wolfenstein  

SUMMARY

In this essay I have reformulated the relationship of the pleasure principle, reality principle, and Nirvana principle. I have linked this conception of the three principles of mental functioning to the idea of the paranoid-schizoid position. And I have attempted to demonstrate the utility of these various notions for illuminating the analyst's sleepiness during analytic sessions.

responseSet.title: Identification and its Vicissitudes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Identification and its Vicissitudes

R. Etchegoyen

R. Etchegoyen

R. Horacio Etchegoyen
Posadas 1580, 13°A
1112 Buenos Aires
Argentina

R. Etchegoyen  

SUMMARY

This paper attempts to understand the vicissitudes of identification within the co-ordinates of narcissism and the object relation.

Firstly the dialectic pair primary identification/secondary

15

identification are studied, and primary narcissism is suggested as the hypothesis which best explains them.

The complex identification processes in the primary scene are considered next and the importance of the introjection of the oedipal parents for the formation of the superego is underlined.

The importance of the structuring function of the introjection and projection mechanisms becomes embodied in the concept of projective identification, which comes to question the postulate of primary narcissism.

The theory of projective–introjective identification is an extremely powerful instrument for explaining phenomena, however it obliges one to accept that the first introjections are radically different from the others. They have nothing to do with mourning but rather with primitive mechanisms which question the subject/object polarity and, so this author believes, spring basically from envy.

Lastly, it is maintained that envy and libido are factors of a dialectic from which the object relation and the earliest processes of identification, previous to the Oedipus complex, proceed at one and the same time.

Male Sexuality: A Clinical Study of Forces that Impede Development

Irma Pick

Irma Pick

Irma Brenman Pick
7 Regents Park Terrace
London NW1 7EE

Irma Pick  

SUMMARY

In this paper an attempt has been made to explore some of the early determinants in the conceptualization of masculinity, femininity and sexual intercourse. It is the area of a patient's failure to negotiate mature male sexuality that is addressed. Clinical material is provided to illustrate the power struggle with which a patient engages as seen in the transference and counter-transference, a dialectic of domination/enslavement. We also see evidence of the beginnings of a new dialectic, the pursuit of an object who will 'take in' and attend to his needs and desires without succumbing to, or responding with, tyranny. This provides a more supporting foundation to move towards a mutually interdependent sexuality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Identification and its Vicissitudes

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer
16 Sunnyridge Road
Harrison
NY 10528

Mark Kanzer  

SUMMARY

The treatment of 'identification' in the American literature is conveniently divided into three eras: (1) The Pioneers (1915–1945). (2) The Synthesizers (1945–1975). (3) Current Commentators (1975–1985). For the Pioneers, concepts and terminology were diffuse, reflecting the diversity of clinical material, methodology and theories accompanying the rapid expansion in analytic experience over a half century. The achievement of an integrated survey by Otto Fenichel in 1945 marked the transition from a fragmentary to a synthesizing approach to identification and its inherent aspects of incorporation and ejection, introjection and projection, internalization and externalization.

The Synthesizers brought to the fore developmental and adaptive viewpoints which traced along longitudinal lines the processes of maturation from the earliest mother—child unit to later phases of life. The differentiation of the self (one's own person) from systemic functioning within the Freudian ego (Hartmann) and a corresponding importance accorded to reciprocal self-object relationships, proved eventful for the conceptualization of identification. A broadening of traditional analytic frameworks with respect to both normal and abnormal forms of behaviour was a correlated achievement in placing identificatory processes between the inner and outer worlds.

The Commentators extended the synthesizing trends, reviewed historical perspectives accordingly, and selected such areas as individual and group identity, the correlation of direct observation with analytic reconstructions and the personal and professional functioning of the analyst himself for special study.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Becoming a Psychoanalyst

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein
San Francisco, California

Robert Wallerstein  

DEAR SIR,

Ordinarily I feel that book reviews ought not to be responded to by authors (or editors). After all, book reviews, whether positive or negative, are statements of more or less expert opinion, and it is incumbent on the journal reader to weigh the review and the reviewer by the same critical standards that should be applied to any other scientific article in the journal. I am however moved to depart from this position and to write, in response to the review by Joseph Slap that you carried of a book which I edited (Becoming a Psychoanalyst), for two reasons: (1) there are major mis-statements of fact in the review that can seriously mislead the potential reader; and (2) the review contains innuendos about matters of motivation that should not be allowed to stand without those attacked having some opportunity to present an alternate perspective upon the same issues.

(May 1985)

499

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Future of Psychoanalysis. Essays in Honor of Heinz Kohut

Edited by Arnold Goldberg. New York: International Universities Press. 1983. Pp. 514.

George Allison

George Allison

George H. Allison
2271 N.E. 51st Street, Seattle, Washington 98105

George Allison  

This collection of papers was originally gathered for presentation at a Festchrift honouring Kohut on his seventieth birthday. His untimely death in October 1981, turned the event into a memorial, and the editor, in his brief preface, speaks of these contributions as being 'addressed to a man whose vigor and vitality as an intellectual leader in psychoanalysis was unmatched'. He feels that it is too soon for 'a proper' memorial volume although these papers constitute a tribute to the importance of Kohut's work.

The collection ranges from all-out enthusiastic papers about Kohut's total oeuvre (e.g. by A. Cooper, B. Brandchaft, E. Wolf, the Tolpins, and A. Ornstein) to the instance of a thoughtful, critical assessment and attempt at integration by Robert Wallerstein. There are no vigorous dissenters represented here, with the exception of Meissner, who in his review of his own and others' points of view about narcissism and the concept of self, does fault Kohut for seeming to pre-empt these areas for his own self psychology and for his failure to articulate his ideas sufficiently with others. There are also a number of articles whose ideas and concepts are only tangentially or inferentially related to Kohut's work per se, like those by Michels, Kanzer, the Litowitzs, and Knapp. The stand of these latter authors as to Kohut's contribution is either unclear, inferential, or not relevant to their material.

The editor has divided the volume into five Parts or sections, which frequently overlap, but which offer some structure to guide the reader as to the main focus of a particular author. The divisions are: Clinical Critique of Self Psychology; Theory; Dreams, Technique and Clinical Theory; Development; and Applied Psychoanalysis. There is some merit in reading the volume in toto since it does represent a potpourri of views and critiques that both extend, reflect and occasionally dissent from Kohut's work. It is worth noting also that many of the papers appear to stem mainly from Kohut's earlier major opus, The Analysis of the Self, while others include his expanded notions from The Restoration of the Self. Which are which is reflected in the references to each article, where one or the other monograph may not be listed. The spirit of this book is clearly related to Kohut's own article, entitled 'The future of psychoanalysis', given as an address originally at his sixtieth birthday celebration and subsequently published (Ornstein, 1978).

[[RunningHead]]

Hidden Selves. Between Theory and Practice in Psychoanalysis

By M. Masud R. Khan. New York: International Universities Press and London: Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. 1983. Pp. 204.

John Kelly

John Kelly

John F. Kelly
650 South Cherry St., Penthouse Suite, Denver, Co. 80222

John Kelly  

This collection of papers by Masud Khan, spanning the years from 1975 to 1982, furthers his explorations of the self and the other recorded in his previous volumes, The Privacy of the Self and Alienation in Perversions. The papers in this current book provocatively question traditional metapsychology and technique, though the answers to the questions he raises are not altogether satisfying or complete. However, there are many novel and unique ideas presented, and the case material makes for fascinating reading, richly and vividly portraying the patients, the analyst, their interactions, and the 'holding space' of the analyst's consultation room within which they work together.

In the preface, the author indicates that he does not intend to satisfy fully, stating, 'this book has no pretentions to offering a "scientific" explication of the other. It is an attempt at sharing the discourse between me and the other. He adds 'encounters take place that ease only gradually in untrust from both sides and enable occasional sharing of the hidden selves of each with the other'. The author does succeed in his proposed intent. The details of his 'encounters', and the work that takes place between analyst and patient that bring about what the author refers to as 'self-cure', allow the reader to become involved in the therapeutic processes and be exposed to the author's notes and associations. He is more explicit in revealing the 'hidden self' of the patient than himself, though there are implications of the latter through his associations, interpretations, and attitudes toward the patients and his therapeutic approaches.

Khan introduces his theme of shared responsibility in therapy of both analyst and patient in this opening chapter entitled, 'Freud and the crises of psychotherapeutic responsibility', first published in 1975. The first half of this chapter is a most interesting and erudite review of an historical perspective on the development of the self through the works of Montaigne, Descartes, Rousseau, and certain Romantic authors, culminating in Freud's contributions of knowing himself through self-discourse. A most interesting point in these early author's autobiographies, not noted by Khan but strengthening his thesis, is their directing their writing to an imagined, particular kind of reader. This phantom reader is an evaluator of their discourses and hopefully accepts the author as a whole person. This theme of those early biographies could be seen as an historical model of the analytic situation.

[[RunningHead]]

On Learning from the Patient

By Patrick Casement. London: Tavistock Publications. 1985. Pp. 244 + x.

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy
The Department of Psychology, University College London
Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT

Peter Fonagy  

Books rarely have titles that describe their contents accurately. Patrick Casement's book is a notable exception. Adopting an attitude that is perhaps most reminiscent of D. W. Winnicott's dedication: 'To my patients who have paid to teach me', the author skilfully outlines the basic principles of psychoanalytic technique not as a set of 'givens', but as lessons to be learned from the careful study of the analytic interchange. In each chapter Casement sets out clinical examples from which his readers can assimilate the basic components of the psychoanalytic method. The reader thus literally learns from the patient in a sense alongside the author.

The first chapter introduces the parsimonious theoretical framework within which the book is couched. It owes little to Matte-Blanco's mathematical model and much to Casement's own imaginative way of explaining the therapeutic relationship. For example, he draws on the process of solving a numerical puzzle to aid the reader's understanding of the process of interpretation. Similarly, concepts such as 'sets', 'subsets' and 'symmetry' are offered as a medium through which the more elaborate notions of transference and countertransference may be absorbed. Casement's main aim is not to impart a thorough grounding in psychoanalytic theory but to acquaint the reader with psychoanalytic technique.

At an early stage Casement introduces the major protagonist of the book, the 'internal supervisor'. The internal supervisor evolves from the internalization of the experience of supervision. The internalization develops to form a system of internal support which in important and subtle ways remains autonomous and separate from the intrapsychic representation of the supervisor. It is the triad of the analyst, the patient and the internal supervisor that forms the core concept of the book. The internal supervisor is responsible for creating the structure which can help the 'therapist to find an inner play-space where the clinical options can be explored (silently or with the patient) rather than remaining blinkered by past thinking that often functions too much like a set of rules' (p. 27).

[[RunningHead]]

The Annual of Psychoanalysis Vol. X.

Edited by the Chicago Institute of Psychoanalysis. New York: International Universities Press. 1982. Pp. 388.

William Bernstein

William Bernstein

William E. Bernstein
4900 Cherry Creek South Drive, Suite F
Denver, Colorado 80222

William Bernstein  

Editing a collection of psychoanalytic papers for annual publication is a task of impressive magnitude. Achieving and maintaining high standards of scholarship and originality increase the complexity of that task. Although there is unevenness in the quality of this collection, the

508

majority of the papers will be of interest to many psychoanalysts.

This annual is dedicated to the memory of Dr Joan Fleming. It contains twenty-one papers arranged in four sections. The four sections are entitled Psychoanalytic Education, Theoretical Studies, Psychoanalytic History, and Applied Psychoanalysis.

The section on psychoanalytic education is edited by Paul Dewald. In his foreword Dewald writes about Joan Fleming's dedication and outstanding contributions to psychoanalytic education. Many of us remember her as our teacher, colleague, and friend.

Some of the writings in this section are refreshingly provocative. Jacob Arlow's psychoanalytic perspective on psychoanalytic education is in that class. Arlow comments on the conservatism of psychoanalytic education. Related to our not having an organized conceptualization of the educational process, an unconscious mythology greatly influences this process. Arlow notes the unending obstacle course of the 'analytic odyssey'. Devising requirements and judging qualifications—admission, progression, graduation, certification—are the central, time consuming topics of interest in the institutes and in the Board on Professional Standards. Within Arlow's metaphor, the training analyst is the one who has overcome all of the obstacles in this long initiation ritual. The fantasied and actual attributed power of the training analyst can be confused easily with an archaic ego ideal.

In Arlow's view, psychoanalytic training, like in any discipline, involves the implanting of a professional attitude, a psychoanalytic identity, as well as the transmission and learning of new knowledge. With our clinging to the past, identification is the primary psychological mechanism by which this identity is achieved. The stronger the repressed ambivalence—idealization and hostility—the stronger the dogmatism. He also remarks on supervision and the classroom. Supervision is all too often an examination where one's self-esteem is challenged, rather than a situation where teaching and learning take place. He objects to an historical approach in the classroom teaching and he notes that learning problems are rarely approached as straightforward challenges to pedagogical procedures. Among his conclusions, Arlow finds that psychoanalysts, like our patients, suffer mainly from reminiscences.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Existentialism and Psychoanalysis

Charles Hanly. New York: International Universities Press. 1979. Pp. 446.

Owen Renik

Owen Renik

Owen Renik
Suite 201, 2300 Sutter Street, San Francisco, CA 94115

Owen Renik  

Dr Hanly has written far and away the best analysis and evaluation currently available of the work of Jean-Paul Sartre. Sartre had an enormous impact upon the intellectual life of the past forty years. His ideas offer a systematic understanding of human experience that flatly rejects the one presented by Freud, yet any number of thinkers and clinicians have sought to wed the two. 'Existential psychotherapy' has been named as an enterprise in its own right; but, perhaps even more important, Sartre's carefully articulated essays place before us in explicit form principles which implicitly guide the thinking of Lacan, Kohut and other influential psychoanalytic revisionists. For all these reasons, Existentialism and Psychoanalysis is a book that psychoanalysts should read.

Hanly's prose is simple and clear. Even readers whose acquaintance with post-war philosophy is limited will be able to follow easily. At the same time, Hanly's exposition is detailed and comprehensive enough to satisfy the most philosophically sophisticated audience. The work of Merleau-Ponty, Heidegger, Kierkegaard, Camus et al. is brought in where appropriate, so that not only a picture of Sartre's thought, but of important variations within an existentialist movement, is given—in so far as that is possible; and background is provided by reference to Plato, Descartes, Hume, Kant, Pascal, Mill, Husserl and Hegel, among others. All in all, it is a tour de force, jam packed without being dense in feeling.

In a first chapter on 'Method', Hanly identifies Sartre's objective, which is to transcend both inductive and deductive reasoning, using them, as Wittgenstein put it, like a ladder which, once climbed, must be thrown away. Existentialism aims at a privileged experience of the irreducibles of the human situation that can only be known through a positive exercise of intuition. Sartre's fundamental postulates are also exposed and discussed, most specifically the assumption that consciousness is a completely autonomous function characterizing all mental acts.

[[RunningHead]]

Changes of Inner Space

Harold Stewart

Harold Stewart

Harold Stewart
16 Ferncroft Avenue
London NW3 7PH

Harold Stewart  

SUMMARY

This paper describes the relationship between the experiential aspects of the concept of inner space and of emptiness. This is based on the change of inner experience of a borderline patient during analysis from one of empty space to one of real space. The analysis is described and the relationship of inner space to schizoid and depressive emptiness elaborated, together with the emergence of a healthy inner space (positive emptiness). The components of the capacity to experience inner space from infancy onwards are enumerated and discussed.
